facts.text: The Great Wall of China stretches over 13,000 miles and is visible from low Earth orbit under ideal conditions.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport has been the world's busiest airport by passenger traffic for over two decades.
Rolling your clothes instead of folding them can save up to 30% more space in your suitcase.
The Orient Express first ran between Paris and Istanbul in 1883, becoming the most famous luxury train in history.
Iceland's Ring Road (Route 1) circles the entire island and covers approximately 828 miles.
Japan's Shinkansen bullet trains have an average delay of less than one minute per year.
The world's first commercial airline flight took place on January 1, 1914, flying between St. Petersburg and Tampa, Florida.
In Thailand, it is considered disrespectful to touch someone's head, as it is regarded as the most sacred part of the body.
The Burj Al Arab in Dubai is one of the tallest hotel buildings in the world, standing at 1,053 feet.
Route 66, also known as the Mother Road, originally ran 2,448 miles from Chicago to Santa Monica.
Singapore's Changi Airport features a butterfly garden, a rooftop swimming pool, and a 130-foot indoor waterfall.
Machu Picchu was built around 1450 AD by the Inca emperor Pachacuti and abandoned roughly 100 years later.
The Trans-Siberian Railway is the longest railway line in the world, spanning 5,772 miles from Moscow to Vladivostok.
Tipping is considered rude in Japan, as excellent service is seen as standard and expected.
The Maldives is the lowest-lying country in the world, with an average ground level of just 4 feet 11 inches above sea level.
Bangkok's street food scene serves over 10 million meals a day and is one of the cheapest places to eat in the world.
World Tourism Day is celebrated every year on September 27, established by the UN World Tourism Organization in 1980.
The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators in its prime.
New Zealand was the last major landmass to be settled by humans, around 1250-1300 AD.
The Pacific Coast Highway in California stretches 656 miles and is considered one of the most scenic drives in the world.
Symphony of the Seas, one of the largest cruise ships ever built, can carry over 6,600 passengers and 2,200 crew members.
Airbnb was founded in 2008 when its creators rented out air mattresses in their San Francisco apartment.
The Grand Tour was a traditional trip through Europe undertaken by young European aristocrats starting in the 17th century.
Dubai International Airport's Terminal 3 is the world's largest airport terminal building by floor space.
Bali's Nyepi Day of Silence involves a complete shutdown of the island, including the airport, for 24 hours.
The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to stand for only 20 years before being dismantled.
Travel insurance typically costs between 4% and 10% of your total trip cost.
The world's longest non-stop commercial flight connects Singapore to New York, covering over 9,500 miles in about 18 hours.
Santorini's iconic white and blue buildings were originally painted that way partly to repel insects.
International Civil Aviation Day is observed on December 7 each year.
The Silk Road was a network of trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean, spanning roughly 4,000 miles.
In many Middle Eastern countries, showing the sole of your shoe is considered highly disrespectful.
Tokyo's Tsukiji Outer Market and its successor Toyosu Market handle over 2,000 tons of seafood daily.
Hostels originated in Germany in 1912 when teacher Richard Schirrmann converted a school into accommodation for traveling students.
The Great Barrier Reef stretches over 1,400 miles along Australia's northeast coast and is visible from space.
Germany's Autobahn has sections with no speed limit, though the recommended speed is 130 km/h (about 81 mph).
The Icehotel in Jukkasjärvi, Sweden, is rebuilt every winter from ice harvested from the nearby Torne River.
Christopher Columbus first reached the Americas on October 12, 1492, landing in the Bahamas.
Paris receives roughly 30 million international visitors per year, making it one of the most visited cities on Earth.
Jet lag affects travelers more severely when flying east because the body finds it harder to advance its internal clock.
The London Underground, opened in 1863, was the world's first underground railway system.
Machu Picchu limits daily visitors to around 4,500 to protect the ancient site from erosion.
The Great Ocean Road in Australia stretches 151 miles along the southeastern coast and was built by returned soldiers after World War I.
In India, it is customary to eat with the right hand, as the left hand is considered unclean.
A gap year before or after university became popular in the UK during the 1960s and has since spread worldwide.
The world's oldest hotel, Nishiyama Onsen Keiunkan in Japan, has been operating since 705 AD.
Singapore Airlines was the first to offer fully flat beds in business class on commercial flights.
Barcelona's La Sagrada Família has been under construction since 1882 and is expected to be completed around 2026.
The world's busiest cruise port is PortMiami, handling over 7 million passengers annually.
National Tourism Day is celebrated in India on January 25 to promote domestic tourism.
Petra, Jordan's Rose City, was carved directly into rose-red cliffs by the Nabataeans over 2,000 years ago.
Currency exchange rates at airports are typically 7-15% worse than rates at local banks or ATMs.
Safari means 'journey' in Swahili, derived from the Arabic word for 'travel.'
The Chunnel connecting England and France runs 31.4 miles under the English Channel and took six years to build.
Rome's Trevi Fountain collects roughly 3,000 euros in coins thrown by tourists each day.
The first modern passport was introduced by King Henry V of England in 1414.
Packing cubes can reduce packing time by up to 50% and keep luggage organized throughout a trip.
Wright Brothers Day commemorates the first successful powered airplane flight on December 17, 1903.
The Northern Lights are best viewed from locations within the auroral zone, such as Tromsø, Norway, or Fairbanks, Alaska.
Venice's historic center has no cars or roads — all transportation is by water or on foot.
The world's deepest hotel room is at the Silvermine in Sala, Sweden, located 509 feet underground.
Australian road trains can be over 160 feet long, consisting of a truck pulling multiple trailers across the Outback.
Michelin stars were first awarded in 1926, and the system was originally designed to encourage French motorists to travel more.
The Glacier Express in Switzerland covers 180 miles through 91 tunnels and over 291 bridges.
In South Korea, it is respectful to use both hands when giving or receiving items from elders.
The world's first travel agency, Thomas Cook, organized its first excursion in 1841 for a temperance meeting.
Dubai's Palm Jumeirah is an artificial island shaped like a palm tree, visible from space.
Backpacking through Southeast Asia is one of the most affordable travel experiences, with daily budgets as low as $20-30.

Helsinki Airport in Finland offers free sauna access to travelers during layovers.
The Camino de Santiago pilgrimage route in Spain has been walked by travelers for over 1,000 years.
Angkor Wat in Cambodia is the largest religious monument in the world, covering over 400 acres.
New York City's Times Square attracts roughly 50 million visitors per year.
The first commercial jet service began on May 2, 1952, when BOAC flew a de Havilland Comet from London to Johannesburg.
In Morocco, bargaining at markets (souks) is expected and considered part of the shopping experience.
The world's tallest hotel is the Gevora Hotel in Dubai, standing at 1,168 feet with 75 floors.
Cruise ships produce as much carbon dioxide as a million cars per year across the entire industry.
The Appalachian Trail spans 2,190 miles from Georgia to Maine and takes most thru-hikers five to seven months to complete.
Japan Rail Pass allows unlimited travel on most JR trains for 7, 14, or 21 days and must be purchased before arriving in Japan.
The world's most visited tourist attraction is the Great Wall of China, with over 10 million visitors annually.
Global Meetings Industry Day is observed on the first Thursday of April each year.
The Panama Canal saves ships roughly 7,800 miles compared to sailing around South America.
Carrying a portable charger on flights ensures devices stay powered during long layovers and travel days.
Marrakech's Jemaa el-Fnaa square transforms from a market by day into an open-air restaurant by night.
Iceland's Blue Lagoon is a man-made geothermal spa fed by water from a nearby geothermal power plant.
The world's first airline loyalty program, AAdvantage, was launched by American Airlines in 1981.
In Ethiopia, the calendar has 13 months, and the country is roughly 7-8 years behind the Gregorian calendar.
Sydney Opera House took 16 years to build and went 14 times over its original budget.
The TSA processes over 2 million passengers per day at U.S. airports.
Volunteering abroad, or voluntourism, has grown into a multi-billion-dollar industry.
The world's shortest commercial flight connects the Scottish islands of Westray and Papa Westray in about 57 seconds.
International Mountain Day is celebrated on December 11 to promote awareness of mountain tourism.
The Ritz Paris was one of the first hotels to provide private bathrooms in every room when it opened in 1898.
Treehouse hotels have become a growing trend, with notable ones in Costa Rica, Sweden, and South Africa.
The Amalfi Coast in Italy stretches 30 miles and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Lonely Planet published its first guidebook in 1973, covering a trip from London to Australia on the cheap.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition became the first to circumnavigate the globe, completing the journey in 1522.
Checking visa requirements at least three months before traveling can prevent costly last-minute issues.
Amsterdam's Schiphol Airport sits 11 feet below sea level, making it one of the lowest airports in the world.
World Travel and Tourism Council estimates that tourism accounts for roughly 10% of global GDP.
The Galápagos Islands inspired Charles Darwin's theory of evolution after his visit in 1835.
Cunard's Queen Mary 2 is the only ocean liner still making regular transatlantic crossings between New York and Southampton.
In Japan, bowing is the standard greeting, with deeper bows showing greater respect.
National Plan for Vacation Day is observed on the last Tuesday of January in the United States.
The world's most expensive hotel suite is the Royal Penthouse Suite at Hotel President Wilson in Geneva, costing over $80,000 per night.
Charles Lindbergh completed the first solo nonstop transatlantic flight on May 21, 1927.
The Great Smoky Mountains National Park is the most visited national park in the United States.
Phở is Vietnam's national dish and can be found at street stalls throughout Hanoi for as little as $1.
The Rocky Mountaineer train in Canada covers routes through the Canadian Rockies and offers dome-car viewing.

Emirates Airlines' first-class suites feature private enclosed cabins, lie-flat beds, and onboard showers.
The Ring of Kerry in Ireland is a 111-mile scenic drive on the Iveragh Peninsula.
In China, exchanging business cards with both hands and studying the card briefly is a sign of respect.
Hawaii was the 50th state admitted to the United States, but its tourism industry was already thriving decades prior.
The world's narrowest street, Spreuerhofstraße in Reutlingen, Germany, is only 12.2 inches wide at its narrowest point.
Amelia Earhart became the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic Ocean on May 20, 1932.
The Taj Mahal took about 22 years to build and required over 20,000 workers.
Drinking tap water is safe in most of Western Europe, Japan, Australia, and Canada, saving travelers money on bottled water.
Kuala Lumpur's Petronas Towers were the tallest buildings in the world from 1998 to 2004.
Carnival Cruise Line carries more passengers than any other cruise line in the world.
The Nazca Lines in Peru are ancient geoglyphs best viewed from the air, created between 500 BC and 500 AD.

In Bhutan, tourism is regulated through a daily tariff to maintain sustainability and limit tourist numbers.

Chiang Mai's Night Bazaar in Thailand is one of the best street markets in Southeast Asia for handicrafts and food.

Marco Polo's travels along the Silk Road in the 13th century helped open trade routes between Europe and Asia.
The world's largest underwater restaurant, Under, is located in Lindesnes, Norway, and seats 40 diners.
Using a VPN while traveling can protect personal data on public Wi-Fi networks at airports and hotels.
London's Heathrow Airport handles over 80 million passengers per year and has five terminals.
The Inca Trail to Machu Picchu requires permits that often sell out months in advance.
Santorini's caldera was formed by one of the largest volcanic eruptions in recorded history around 1600 BC.

The Age of Exploration, spanning the 15th to 17th centuries, saw European explorers reach the Americas, Africa, and Asia.

Bali, Indonesia, attracts over 6 million international tourists annually.

The Transcontinental Railroad in the United States was completed on May 10, 1869.

Packing a sarong is a versatile travel hack — it can serve as a towel, blanket, skirt, or privacy screen.
The world's highest bungee jump is at the Bloukrans Bridge in South Africa, at 709 feet.
In many Latin American countries, arriving exactly on time to social events is considered too early.
The Canterbury Tales by Geoffrey Chaucer is one of the earliest literary works centered on a pilgrimage journey.
Glacier National Park in Montana has lost over two-thirds of its glaciers since the mid-1800s due to climate change.
The world's first commercial airline, DELAG, was founded in Germany in 1909 to operate Zeppelin flights.
Abu Simbel temples in Egypt were relocated in the 1960s to save them from flooding caused by the Aswan High Dam.
Using public transportation rather than taxis can save travelers up to 80% on urban transport costs.
The world's oldest continuously operating airport is College Park Airport in Maryland, opened in 1909.
Salar de Uyuni in Bolivia is the world's largest salt flat, spanning over 4,000 square miles.
In Korea, pouring your own drink is considered rude — someone else should always pour for you.
Cinque Terre in Italy consists of five colorful fishing villages perched along the Ligurian coast.
The world's busiest border crossing is between San Diego, California, and Tijuana, Mexico.
Carrying a universal sink plug allows hand-washing clothes in any accommodation.
The Terracotta Army in Xi'an, China, consists of over 8,000 soldiers, 130 chariots, and 670 horses.
The Costa Concordia disaster in 2012 led to major improvements in cruise ship safety regulations.
The Erie Canal, completed in 1825, opened the Great Lakes region to travel and trade from the East Coast.
International Day for Monuments and Sites was proclaimed by UNESCO on April 18, 1982.
The world's longest tunnel, the Gotthard Base Tunnel in Switzerland, stretches 35.5 miles under the Alps.
Havana, Cuba, is home to one of the best-preserved collections of Spanish colonial architecture in the Americas.
In Scandinavia, the concept of 'allemansrätten' (right to roam) allows travelers to hike and camp almost anywhere in nature.
The Bermuda Triangle, between Miami, Bermuda, and Puerto Rico, is one of the most mythologized travel regions in the world.
Osaka, Japan, is nicknamed 'the kitchen of Japan' for its legendary street food culture.
The first package holiday was organized by Thomas Cook in 1841 for a train trip from Leicester to Loughborough.
Myanmar's Bagan plain contains over 2,000 Buddhist temples and pagodas spread across 26 square miles.
Travel bloggers and influencers have turned destination marketing into a multi-billion-dollar digital industry.
The ancient Incan road system, Qhapaq Ñan, stretched over 24,000 miles across six South American countries.
In South Korea, age is calculated differently — everyone is considered one year old at birth.
The world's oldest known map is a Babylonian clay tablet from around 600 BC showing Mesopotamia and surrounding regions.
Fiji has over 330 islands, but only about 110 are permanently inhabited.
The Harbour Bridge in Sydney, Australia, is the widest long-span bridge in the world and can be climbed by tourists.
The world's first nonstop round-the-world flight without refueling was completed by the Rutan Voyager on December 23, 1986.
Montenegro's Bay of Kotor is often called Europe's southernmost fjord and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Wearing bright colors on safari can scare away wildlife — neutral earth tones are strongly recommended.
The Polynesian Triangle encompasses Hawaii, New Zealand, and Easter Island, connected by ancient seafaring routes.
Madrid's Sobrino de Botín, founded in 1725, holds the Guinness World Record as the oldest continuously operating restaurant.
George Stephenson's Rocket locomotive won the Rainhill Trials on October 14, 1829, launching the age of rail travel.
The world's longest overwater bungalow walkway is at the Soneva Jani resort in the Maldives, stretching over half a mile.
